metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

`auto-executed-tool-call` (scenario-meanings.md): the agent invokes one or more tools mid-turn, every call auto-resolves with no user approval, and the turn completes â€” the user-observable arc is `ready â†’ working â†’ ready` and state NEVER enters `waiting`. Primitive `tool_calls`; requires `headless_mode` and `tool_calls`. The coverage matrix records pi/auto-executed-tool-call as yes/yes.

**agent_supports: yes.** Pi invokes its read/shell tool autonomously and runs it without an approval gate. A prompt that asks pi to read a file produces a real tool round-trip (toolCall + toolResult) that auto-resolves without ever blocking on the user, then completes the turn.

**daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready.** The pi parser translates `toolCall` / `toolResult` message events into the tailer's tool-use / tool-result records and keeps the session `working` across them; the final assistant message's `stopReason=stop` maps to `turn_done`, settling `working â†’ ready` via the primary turn-end path. Pi has no permission hooks, so no `waiting` transition is emitted â€” matching the scenario's negative invariant. Confirmed against the committed pi regression fixture for the sibling cell (full-lifecycle-toolcall, same coverage_id): the transcript carries toolCall + toolResult events and the events arc is `ready â†’ working â†’ ready` with zero `waiting` transitions.

## Recipe-shape note (SCRIPT / interactive)

Recorded with the INTERACTIVE pi driver (drive-pi-interactive.sh, a tmux REPL) rather than headless `pi --print`. WHY: the sibling headless cell full-lifecycle-toolcall (same coverage_id) drifted 2/5 because `pi --print` exits the moment it emits the final reply, so the daemon's scanner can miss the terminal workingâ†’ready settle before process_exited (concern C6). Keeping pi alive in the REPL across one send + wait_turn lets the daemon observe the settle cleanly; the single tmux teardown then yields one clean process_exited. The prompt asks pi to read a file created in setup and report its first line, forcing exactly one auto-executed read tool call inside the working span. C7 / session-identity: the live REPL emits a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready at launch (it bridges the gap until the UUID-keyed rollout transcript appears), so session_birth â€” which only asserts 'a session appears in ready within 2s of launch' â€” legitimately matches that pre-session. The turn arc, pid_bind and teardown all pin to the rollout session via the first-working anchor + same_session_as, so the pre-session can never satisfy them. The phase DSL has no tool-event form, so the tool call is asserted via the working span it sustains plus the transcript's toolCall/toolResult events, not a dedicated phase.
caveats: claudecode's variant of this scenario can end in waiting via a PostToolUse permission hook (the top-level scenarios.json verify.has_waiting_state is claudecode-shaped); pi has no such hook, so the pi arc is readyâ†’workingâ†’ready and the spec asserts a no-waiting invariant rather than has_waiting_state.
Pi cannot accept a pre-assigned UUID â€” it generates one itself. The interactive driver discovers the UUID from the session header on line 1 of the freshly-created rollout transcript.

Because the live REPL bridges with a transient proc-<PID> pre-session, the turn phase anchors to recording start with NO session pin so the matcher binds the FIRST working (necessarily the rollout session; the pre-session never goes working); every later phase pins to it via same_session_as.

sources.note: Identity.Name=pi, Source variant FilesUnderRoot under ~/.pi/agent/sessions/, ExactName{Name: "pi"} process matcher.
toolCall/toolResult â†’ tool-use/tool-result; stopReason=stop on the final assistant event maps to turn_done (workingâ†’ready). No permission gate â†’ no waiting.
Sibling cell (same coverage_id): carries toolCall + toolResult events; events arc is readyâ†’workingâ†’ready with no waiting transition.
auto-executed-tool-call canonical flow: readyâ†’workingâ†’ready, never enters waiting.
Interactive tmux REPL driver; supports send / wait_turn / interrupt / sleep, keeps the rollout alive so the daemon observes the workingâ†’ready settle before teardown.

preconditions: pi CLI on PATH and authenticated (pi runs against its configured model out-of-band)
tmux available â€” the interactive driver runs pi in a tmux REPL so it stays alive across the turn
irrlichd is recording â€” either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record
setup: The interactive driver (drive-pi-interactive.sh) launches pi in a fresh per-run cwd inside a tmux session, waits for the REPL prompt, then sends the prompt
pi writes one JSONL rollout file per session under ~/.pi/agent/sessions/<cwd-slug>/ on launch (FilesUnderRoot, non-lazy); the driver resolves that file and extracts the UUID from line 1

WHY interactive, not headless: the sibling headless cell full-lifecycle-toolcall (same coverage_id) drifts because `pi --print` exits the instant it emits the final reply, so the daemon can miss the terminal workingâ†’ready settle before process_exited (concern C6). Keeping pi alive in the REPL across one send + wait_turn lets the daemon observe the settle cleanly, and the single tmux teardown then yields one clean process_exited
The send bumps the expected-turn counter; wait_turn blocks until the rollout shows a completed assistant turn, so the trailing sleep never races a still-running tool round-trip

script.text: Run the shell command 'echo hello' using your bash tool, then reply with exactly: done

verify: events.jsonl contains a transcript_new for the rollout session born ready within ~2s of REPL launch (a transient proc-<PID> pre-session may also appear; the turn arc, pid_discovered and teardown land on the UUID-keyed rollout session)
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the rollout session_id
events.jsonl contains exactly one state_transitionâ†’working / â†’ready arc on the rollout session_id, sustained across the auto-executed bash call
transcript.jsonl contains a toolCall / toolResult pair for the echo command (the auto-executed, ungated tool call â€” pi has no permission hook)
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to waiting for the rollout session at any point (the call auto-executes; pi never gates)
the rollout session's final live state is ready before the driver tears the REPL down
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the rollout session at REPL teardown
